[[Dick Smith (rugby league)|Dick Smith]] (brother) }} }} '''Jack Smith''' was a rugby league player who represented [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] in eleven matches in the 1938 tour of Australia and 1939 aborted tour of England. He predominantly played at fullback but also on the wing and occasionally at centre and standoff. In the process he became the 265th player to represent New Zealand. He also represented [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]], the [[North Island rugby league team|North Island]], Auckland Pākehā, and played for several rugby teams in the Middle East while serving in World War 2. He was a member of the [[North Shore Albions]] club (also named Devonport United). His brother was [[Dick Smith (rugby league)|Dick Smith]], the New Zealand rugby league representative in 1932, they were both the nephews of the well known sportsman of the 1890s–1910s [[George William Smith (sportsman)|George Smith]]. ==Early life== Jack Smith was born Charles Ernest Smith but became known as Jack. He was born on 1 February 1918, to Walton Smith (1883-1938) and Clara Evelyn Smith (née Green) (1890-). He had two older brothers named Richard Frederick Smith (1909-1931), and Walton Victor Roy Smith (1911-1964), though Walton was better known as [[Dick Smith (rugby league)|Dick Smith]].{{cite news | title = Reason Of Success | page = 6 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 223 | date = 1941-09-20 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410920.2.180.62 | accessdate = 2021-09-20}} Dick had also played for [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] in 1932 in three tests against England. Their uncle (Walton's brother) was [[George William Smith (sportsman)|George Smith]], the famous sportsman who represented [[New Zealand national rugby union team|New Zealand]] in rugby union, rugby league, as well as being a champion runner.{{cite news | title = Death At Devonport/Father Of Footballers | page = 18 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 124 | date = 1938-05-28 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380528.2.146 | accessdate = 2021-09-20}} ==Playing career== ===North Shore Albions (Devonport United) rugby league=== ====Juniors (1935–36)==== Jack Smith began playing for [[North Shore Albions]], known as Devonport United at the time, in the mid 1930s. At the start of the 1936 season at their annual meeting he was presented with a medal for being the best player in their seventh grade team for 1935.{{cite news | title = Devonport United Club/Sixteenth Annual Meeting | page = 24 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVII, Issue 61 | date = 1936-03-12 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19360312.2.232 | accessdate = 2024-11-23}} He was still attending [[Takapuna Grammar School]] at the time and playing for their first fifteen rugby side. On July 24 they traveled to [[Whangārei]] to play Whangarei High School at Kensington Oval with Smith playing at fullback. Whangarei won easily 20-9.{{cite news | title = Takapuna School Visit | page = 6 | publisher = Northern Advocate, 24 July 1936, Page 6 | date = 1936-07-24 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NA19360724.2.34 | accessdate = 2024-11-23}} While for his North Shore rugby league side he played in their third grade team in 1936 and won a trophy for his play for them which was presented at the 1937 annual meeting.{{cite news | title = Devonport United | page = 23 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 65 | date = 1937-03-18 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370318.2.185.2 | accessdate = 2024-11-23}} ===1937 season=== Jack Smith made his senior debut at the start of the [[1937 Auckland Rugby League season|1937]] season in the [[Auckland Rugby League]] competition. He went on to play 19 matches, scoring 7 tries and kicking 39 goals. Smith was named at centre, with [[Len Scott]] outside him on the wing and [[Verdun Scott]] on the other wing for their preliminary round match against [[Newton Rangers]] on April 17.{{cite news | title = North Shore Albions | page = 12 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 89 | date = 1937-04-16 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370416.2.130 | accessdate = 2024-11-23}} In a 34-8 win Smith was “outstanding” and an “enterprising centre”.{{cite news | title = Rangers Down To Shore | page = 15 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 91 | date = 1937-04-19 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370419.2.155.2 | accessdate = 2024-11-23}} The [[New Zealand Herald]] wrote that “J. Smith is a promoted player, likely to be given a chance. He has speed and is clever on attack”.{{cite news | title = The Other Clubs | page = 21 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22708 | date = 1937-04-21 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370421.2.226.2 | accessdate = 2024-11-23}} On April 24 they beat [[Marist Saints|Marist Old Boys]] 24-15 with Smith kicking a conversion and three penalties. Len Scott scored a try after “a great piece of work by Smith” who gave Scott good support throughout the match, playing “a good game at centre”.{{cite news | title = Seasiders' Success | page = 14 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 97 | date = 1937-04-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370426.2.138 | accessdate = 2024-11-23}}{{cite news | title = Weakness In Backs | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22712 | date = 1937-04-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370426.2.170.8 | accessdate = 2024-11-23}} The Herald wrote a short piece about him during the week headed “Out Standing Player” which said “a player who caught the eye for North Shore against Marist in the principal attraction last Saturday was J. Smith, the centre three-quarter. He showed ability above the average, making clever openings and running his supports into good positions. Smith is also a fine place-kick”.{{cite news | title = Outstanding Player | page = 18 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22714 | date = 1937-04-28 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370428.2.211.2 | accessdate = 2024-11-23}} In North Shore’s first Fox Memorial Shield game of the season Smith kicked 2 conversions and a penalty in a 19-12 loss to [[Manukau Magpies|Manukau]].{{cite news | title = Bright Display | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22718 | date = 1937-05-04 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370503.2.175.6 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} He repeated the feat a week later on May 8 when North Shore beat [[Richmond Rovers]] 15-13 in the feature match at [[Carlaw Park]].{{cite news | title = Two Points Margin | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22724 | date = 1937-05-10 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370510.2.143.13 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} In a round 3 win over [[Marist Saints|Marist Old Boys]] by 6 points to 5, Smith scored one of their tries. The Auckland Star said that Smith along with the other inside backs showed “dash and daring”.{{cite news | title = Shore's Lucky Victory | page = 13 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 115 | date = 1937-05-17 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370517.2.119.4 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} Then against Ponsonby he kicked three penalties in an 8-8 draw with the newspaper saying that he “had his kicking boots on with a vengeance”.{{cite news | title = Tries Versus Goals | page = 13 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 121 | date = 1937-05-24 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370524.2.171 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} [[File:1937 portrait photo aged 19.png|thumb|left|Jack Smith aged 19 in his debut season.]]The Herald wrote that “the value of a good place-kick to a team was well exemplified in the North Shore-Ponsonby match when J. Smith, the North Shore centre, kicked three excellent penalty goals”.{{cite news | title = An Interesting Position | page = 20 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22738 | date = 1937-05-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370526.2.233.5 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} Then two weeks later in Round 6 Smith scored all nine of North Shore’s points in a 10-9 loss to [[City Rovers]]. His points came through a try, a conversion, and two penalties. He played at five eighths and “was the best of the backs” for North Shore.{{cite news | title = Narrow Victory/North Shore Beaten | page = 16 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22748 | date = 1937-06-07 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370607.2.173.13 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} The Auckland Star said “Smith was the outstanding back”.{{cite news | title = City Rovers Prevail | page = 12 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 133 | date = 1937-06-07 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370607.2.132.4 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} As well as kicking a “fine goal” he “narrowly missed another, which would have given North Shore the game”.{{cite news | title = City Improves | page = 17 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22750 | date = 1937-06-09 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370609.2.192.2 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} He then moved back to the centre position for North Shore’s 13-0 win over [[Newton Rangers]] on June 12. He kicked a conversion and a penalty with it written that “in Smith, the Shore team have a very promising centre and a goal kicker”.{{cite news | title = Brilliant Full-Backs | page = 15 | publisher = Auckland Star, Issue LXVIII | date = 1937-06-14 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370614.2.144.6 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} He was “the best of the North Shore backs, being prominent on attack”.{{cite news | title = Good Back Play/North Shore In Form | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22754 | date = 1937-06-14 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370614.2.162.5 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} North Shore had a break with representative matches on and travelled to Huntly to play the local side on June 19 with North Shore winning 23-5.{{cite news | title =League Tours | page = 12 | work=[[Auckland Star]]| volume=LXVIII| issue=145 | date = 1937-06-21 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370621.2.124 | access-date = 2021-05-11}} After senior games resumed in Auckland on July 3 Smith kicked a lone conversion in a heavy 31-11 loss to Manukau at Carlaw Park.{{cite news | title = Manukau Improves | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22772 | date = 1937-07-05 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370705.2.161.16 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} North Shore then lost again, to Richmond 13-11 though Smith “at five eighths got the try of the match with a great run from halfway, and in addition landed two penalty goals”.{{cite news | title = Leaders Scrape Home | page = 12 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 163 | date = 1937-07-12 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370712.2.178 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} Smith and Zane were singled out for their outstanding defence which kept [[Noel Bickerton]] and [[Ted Mincham]] in check. Smith “caught Mincham, centre, in possession” on many occasions. During the game he “gathered in the loose and cleverly beat MIncham and Bickerton and after a fast run for 40 yards gave Furnell a perfect “dummy” to score near the posts”.{{cite news | title = North Shore Unlucky | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22778 | date = 1937-07-12 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370712.2.161.5 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}}{{cite news | title = Excellent Defence | page = 20 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22780 | date = 1937-07-14 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370714.2.207.10 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} In a 9-7 win over City on July 17 “Smith and [[Verdun Scott|V. Scott]] appeared to be the best”.{{cite news | title = A Scrappy Game | page = 15 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 169 | date = 1937-07-19 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370719.2.153.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} He missed a try when he “made a nice dash and had [Cyril] Wiberg beaten for pace but V. Scott collided with Smith in attempting a scissors pass”. The Herald said that Smith and Zane were the best of their backs.{{cite news | title = Close Contest | page = 17 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22784 | date = 1937-07-19 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370719.2.191.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-25}} Smith missed their next match with Marist after suffering from a “poisoned hand” and was still not listed in their side for their July 31 match against Newton with North Shore losing both games.{{cite news | title = Marist Avenge Defeat | page = 13 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 175 | date = 1937-06-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370726.2.145 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} He returned to play on August 21 against [[Ponsonby Ponies|Ponsonby]] in a 23-9 loss. He kicked a conversion and two penalties and was said to be “the best of the backs, with fine all round play, and as a [[place kick]] he is about the most reliable club man in Auckland”.{{cite news | title = Ponsonby Superior | page = 15 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 199 | date = 1937-08-23 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370823.2.153.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} Then playing at centre in a high scoring loss to [[Mount Albert Lions|Mount Albert United]] by 38 to 22 Smith scored a try and kicked 7 goals. He “gave a fine all-round display”.{{cite news | title = Sixty Points Shared | page = 13 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 205 | date = 1937-08-30 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370830.2.134 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} The Herald wrote that “Smith had a successful day, securing many points for North Shore from place kicks and also scoring a good try”.{{cite news | title = High Scoring Game | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22820 | date = 1937-08-30 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370830.2.153.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} With the championship now finished North Shore played Ponsonby in round 1 of the Roope Rooster and were eliminated following a 23-21 loss. Smith kicked 5 goals and the Star wrote “the fine all-round play of Smith in the centre was of great value to his side…”.{{cite news | title = Ponsonby Three-Quarters | page = 13 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 211 | date = 1937-09-06 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370906.2.134 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} North Shore now moved into the Phelan Shield competition and Smith scored 2 tries and kicked 3 more goals in a 15-14 win against Richmond. He reportedly “topped his 100 points for the season” in the match.{{cite news | title = Surprise For Richmond | page = 12 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 217 | date = 1937-09-13 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370913.2.138.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} His “good goal kicking… gave North Shore the advantage”.{{cite news | title = North Shore Surprises | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22832 | date = 1937-09-13 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370913.2.147.8 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} Then in the semi final “a feature of the game was the splendid play of J. Smith, the North Shore centre. Late in the second half Manukau played with renewed vigour and looked likely to turn the tables. Smith however, scored a fine try and placed the result beyond doubt”.{{cite news | title = North Shore Bears Manukau | page = 16 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22838 | date = 1937-09-20 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370920.2.150.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} North Shore went on to win the Phelan Shield final 22-18 against Mount Albert on October 2. Smith kicked two goals including a go ahead penalty towards the end of the game. He played centre and “was seen at his best”.{{cite news | title = Spectacular Display | page = 15 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 235 | date = 1937-10-04 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19371004.2.174 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} The Herald said that Smith at centre was “outstanding” and he “figured in most of the back movements and gave his best display to date” though he “had a day off at goal kicking”.{{cite news | title = North Shore Success | page = 16 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22850 | date = 1937-10-04 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19371004.2.160.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} In a summary of the year the Herald wrote that Smith had shown “good form” at centre three-quarter during the year.{{cite news | title = Promising North Shore Team | page = 22 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXIV, Issue 22852 | date = 1937-10-06 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19371006.2.209.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} It was also announced that E Bennett of Takapuna was donating a trophy to present to Jack Smith for his point scoring feats for the season where he was said to have scored 132 points in all games. He was only beaten by [[John Anderson (rugby league)|John Anderson]] of Marist who had scored 148. It had been very rare for players to score more than 100 points in club matches in Auckland to this point and rarer still for two players to achieve the feat.{{cite news | title = Two Special Trophies | page = 8 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 239 | date = 1937-10-08 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19371008.2.90 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}}{{cite news | title = Varied Programme | page = 15 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 241 | date = 1937-10-11 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19371011.2.153 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} ====Auckland Selection==== [[File:Jack Smith and John Anderson Point trophies.png|thumb|left]]Smith’s achievements for the season saw him chosen in the 17 player [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]] squad to train to play against New Zealand Māori on October 9.{{cite news | title = Game Against Maoris | page = 16 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 231 | date = 1937-09-29 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19370929.2.145 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} When the squad was reduced to 15 he was named as one of the backs.{{cite news | title = Maoris To Meet Pakehas | page = 23 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXVIII, Issue 237 | date = 1937-10-06 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19371006.2.192 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} The Auckland (Pākehā) team was beaten 43-21 by the Māori side. During the afternoon Smith was presented with his trophy along with Anderson with a photograph recording the moment published in the New Zealand Herald.{{cite news | title = Attractive Programme | page = 15 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Issue 22856 | date = 1937-10-11 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19371011.2.162.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}}{{cite news | title = High Rugby League Scorers Presented With Trophies | page = 15 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Issue 22856 | date = 1937-10-11 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19371011.2.162.3.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-27}} ===1938 season=== The [[1938 Auckland Rugby League season|1938]] season saw Jack Smith make his test debut for [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]]. He only played ten matches for North Shore as he spent two months touring [[Australia]] with the New Zealand side in the middle part of the season. In those ten matches he did not score a try but did kick 39 goals. He was selected to play for [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]] in one match, and also made the [[North Island rugby league team|North Island]] side for their inter-island match. [[File:Jack Smith portrait.png|thumb]]Joining him in the North Shore side for the season was his brother [[Dick Smith (rugby league)|Dick Smith]] who had come out of retirement after a season with [[Newton Rangers|Newton]] in 1936, having played for the North Shore seniors prior to that from [[1931 Auckland Rugby League season|1931]] to [[1935 Auckland Rugby League season|1935]].{{cite news | title = North Shore Albion | page = 15 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 83 | date = 1938-04-08 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380408.2.157.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} They lost their first round Fox Memorial match with [[Marist Saints|Marist]] 23 to 7 at Carlaw Park. Jack kicked “two splendid goals” in the first half to make the score close and was said to have “played a good game at centre three-quarter, while R [Dick] Smith showed patches of his old form”.{{cite news | title = Marist Impresses | page = 20 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23010 | date = 1938-04-11 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380411.2.180.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} The [[Auckland Star]] said that in [Jack] Smith “they have a good centre, who, in addition, is a remarkably fine place-kick”.{{cite news | title = Auspicious Start | page = 19 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 86 | date = 1938-04-12 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380412.2.151 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} The side was being coached by Australian [[Bob Banham]] who had been brought across the [[Tasman Sea|Tasman]] to develop senior players in the [[Auckland]] area. He initially was appointed to the North Shore club which had several young backs. It was reported that “both Smith brothers were associated in some nice play with the coach, and their play will be more effective once movements have been discussed with Banham”.{{cite news | title = Opinion Of Coach | page = 25 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23012 | date = 1938-04-13 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380413.2.206 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} North Shore lost their next match against Manukau 26 to 21 with Smith kicking five goals with the newspapers describing him as “a big asset”, and his brother Dick kicked one.{{cite news | title = Another Five-Point Win | page = 14 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 90 | date = 1938-04-18 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380418.2.163 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} He was also said to have “played a good game at fullback and often raced his backs into scoring positions”.{{cite news | title = Fast Display | page = 13 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23015 | date = 1938-04-18 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380418.2.147 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} Their first win followed with a 15-11 defeat of Mount Albert on April 23 with Smith kicking three goals and “played a good game”.{{cite news | title = Games In The Rain | page = 19 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 96 | date = 1938-04-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380426.2.165 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}}{{cite news | title = North Shore Wins | page = 18 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23021 | date = 1938-04-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380426.2.203 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} It was said that “J. Smith, usually a five eighths, was a success at fullback. Not only did Smith open up the play for the three quarters, but his tackling also compared with anything seen on the ground”.{{cite news | title = Popular Victory | page = 22 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23022 | date = 1938-04-27 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380427.2.215 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} North Shore then lost a controversial match 20-19 to Richmond with the game ordered to be replayed at a later date if required. An obstruction try was ruled but the referee allowed the conversion from where the ball was placed afterwards rather than in line with where the play was when he blew his whistle. Smith kicked five goals for North Shore though it was reported that “North Shore started with great dash and had J. Smith been in his best kicking form the result would have been placed beyond doubt in the first spell”. He had an “occasional kicking duel” with Frank Furnell on the Richmond side. Smith also “played a cool game at fullback for North Shore. His handling and kicking were rarely at fault, while he tackled soundly” and “continued to show good form”.{{cite news | title = Several Mistakes | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23026 | date = 1938-05-02 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380502.2.162 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}}{{cite news | title = Richmond's Win/Shore Dissatisfied | page = 16 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 102 | date = 1938-05-03 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380503.2.132 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} Another 3 goals followed in their 24-16 win over [[City Rovers|City]] in round 5.{{cite news | title = Shore Combined Better | page = 16 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 107 | date = 1938-05-09 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380509.2.149.3 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} He “raced his backs into position”, and “played a splendid game at fullback and made several nice openings. His goal kicking was a feature of the game”.{{cite news | title = North Shore Superior | page = 16 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23032 | date = 1938-05-09 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380509.2.157 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} Once again Smith showed his kicking prowess in a 20-15 win against [[Papakura Sea Eagles|Papakura]] in round 6 when he converted both of their tries and kicked five penalties. He was said to have had “a goal kicking duel” with J. McInnes of Papakura who kicked six goal himself.{{cite news | title = Remarkable Goal Kicking | page = 16 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 113 | date = 1938-05-16 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380516.2.173 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} Smith, at full back was said to have “played a splendid game on attack, and rarely failed to start a passing bout…”.{{cite news | title = Great Struggle/Goal-Kicking A Feature | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23038 | date = 1938-05-16 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380516.2.142 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} ====Auckland selection==== On May 12 Smith was named at fullback in the [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]] team to play against a Rest of the North Island side on May 18 at [[Carlaw Park]].{{cite news | title = Representative Trial/Auckland Team Selected | page = 11 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23035 | date = 1938-05-12 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380512.2.44 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} The Auckland Star said that “consistently good play and marked goal kicking ability has resulted in J. Smith, the North Shore player, getting the place of full-back in the Auckland team”.{{cite news | title = Tomorrow's Trial | page = 15 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 114 | date = 1938-05-17 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380517.2.120.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} Auckland thrashed their opponent 67-14 with Smith converting seven tries and [[John Anderson (rugby league)|John Anderson]] four of their 14 tries. It was said that “little fault could be found with Smith at fullback, who fielded well and kicked with good judgement”.{{cite news | title = Auckland's Easy Victory | page = 11 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23041 | date = 1938-05-19 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380519.2.43 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} ====North Island selection==== After Auckland’s easy win Smith was named by selectors [[Thomas McClymont]], [[Hec Brisbane]], and [[Gordon Hooker]] in the [[North Island rugby league team|North Island]] to play the [[South Island rugby league team|South Island]].{{cite news | title = Inter-Island Game | page = 17 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23041 | date = 1938-05-19 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380519.2.171 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} The North Island trounced the South Island 55-2 with Smith converting 10 of their 12 tries and also adding a penalty. Unsurprisingly most of his conversions were “from in front”. He was said to have “played an outstanding game” at fullback where “he handled and kicked well, and was very sound on defence, while he showed much cleverness in running his backs into attacking positions”.{{cite news | title = Smith's Outstanding Game | page = 13 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23044 | date = 1938-05-23 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380523.2.156.1 | accessdate = 2024-11-28}} ====New Zealand selection for Australian Tour (1938)==== [[File:J Smith 1938 portrait.png|thumb|left]][[File:NZ RL team to tour Australia, 1938.png|thumb|right|New Zealand rugby league team profile pictures of the touring team to Australia in 1938.]]After the inter-island match Jack Smith was chosen for the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] team on their nine match tour of [[Australia]]. The selectors were [[Hec Brisbane]], [[Thomas McClymont|Scotty McClymont]], and [[Jim Amos (rugby league)|Jim Amos]]. Smith was named as one of the two touring full backs with the other being the famous [[George Nēpia]], though he was later unable to tour and was replaced by [[Jack Hemi]]. The Star newspaper said “the inclusion of J. Smith… is fully justified. The young North Shore player has steadily made his way to the top this season and his remarkable goal kicking ability has given him an added claim for recognition”. Smith was aged just 20 at the time.{{cite news | title = Won His Way | page = 15 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 119 | date = 1938-05-23 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380523.2.164 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} [[File:NZ team photo to visit Australia in 1938.png|thumb|right|NZ team at a civic reception. Smith is in the front row, slightly to the right of centre]]Tragedy struck Smith’s family when his father died just five days after his naming in the touring side. Walton was aged 55 years old. The notice in the newspaper said “The death occurred at [[Devonport, New Zealand|Devonport]] this morning of Mr. Walter [sic] Smith, a well-known resident, and father of [[Dick Smith (rugby league)|R. Smith]] and J. Smith, two well-known North Shore rugby league players, the latter being the full back chosen for the New Zealand league team to tour Australia. Mr. Smith was a brother of [[George William Smith (sportsman)|George Smith]], the former well-known Auckland footballer, who was a member of the original [[New Zealand national rugby union team|All Black]] team which toured [[United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland|Britain]] and [[Canada]]. Members of the North Shore team, in their match with [[Ponsonby Ponies|Ponsonby]] this afternoon, at Devonport, wore white armlets”. Smith did not play in the match.{{cite news | title = Death At Devonport/Father Of Footballers | page = 18 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 124 | date = 1938-05-28 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380528.2.146 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} Dick sent the [[Auckland Rugby League]] a thank you letter for their sympathy shown towards the family.{{cite news | title = League Football | page = 28 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 128 | date = 1938-06-02 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380602.2.194 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} His funeral was held on May 30 and saw the New Zealand team in attendance.{{cite news | title = Rugby League Team | page = 10 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23051 | date = 1938-05-31 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380531.2.109 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} [[File:NZ Team 1938.png|450px|left|NZ Team 1938]]The New Zealand team departed [[Auckland]] on board the Canadian – Australasian liner, [[RMS Niagara]] on the evening of 31 May.{{cite news | title =Yesterday's Departures | page = 8 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23053 | date = 2 June 1938 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380602.2.24 | accessdate = 10 July 2024}} The same day they had also been given a civic farewell by the mayor, [[Ernest Davis (brewer)|Sir Ernest Davis]] at the [[Auckland Town Hall]] at noon following a morning tea at George Court, Limited. While the night before they had a farewell ball at the Peter Pan cabaret.{{cite news | title =Rugby League Team | page = 10 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23051 | date = 31 May 1938 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380531.2.109 | accessdate = 10 July 2024}}[[File:NIAGARA of London leaving Sydney for Vancouver.jpg|thumb|The Niagara Ship]] In comments on the New Zealand players the [[Sydney Morning Herald]] noted that Smith “is a very fine place kick” and mentioned his relationship with George Smith.{{cite news | title = New Zealand Team Selected | page = 15 | publisher = The Sydney Morning Herald (NSW : 1842 - 1954) | date = 1938-05-23 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article17467796 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} [[The Sun (Sydney)]] newspaper stated that Smith “will most likely play in the big games [and] should show Australians some brilliant goal kicking”.{{cite news | title = N.Z. League's Tour | page = 18 | publisher = The Sun (Sydney, NSW : 1910 - 1954) | date = 1938-05-23 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article231023989 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} He was also “fully expected to make good” on the tour in a piece by an Auckland correspondent that appeared in [[The Telegraph (Brisbane)]].{{cite news | title = Strong N.Z. League Team to Tour Australia | page = 14 | publisher = The Telegraph (Brisbane, Qld. : 1872 - 1947) | date = 1938-05-23 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article184126517 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} The Courier-Mail in an article by Pat Frawley noted that Smith was “the lightest man” in the New Zealand squad weighing 10 stone. Though he in fact weighed 10 stone 9 pounds and halfback [[Wilfred Brimble]] weighed just 10 stone 3 pounds.{{cite news | title = What Sportsmen Are Saying | page = 14 | publisher = The Courier-Mail (Brisbane, Qld. : 1933 - 1954) | date = 1938-05-31 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article40996676 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} While The [[Labor Daily]] (Sydney) said that Smith had “advanced steadily this season, as the result of tuition from ex-[[South Sydney Rabbitohs|South Sydney]] back, Bobby Banham”.{{cite news | title = N.Z. & Rugby League Rules | page = 12 | publisher = The Labor Daily (Sydney, NSW : 1924 - 1938) | date = 1938-06-01 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article236404752 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} An Auckland correspondent in a Sydney Morning Herald article said that Smith “was originally a centre-threequarter, but is ideally suited to the position of custodian”.{{cite news | title = Notes About Players | page = 15 | publisher = The Sydney Morning Herald (NSW : 1842 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-02 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article17470224 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} The Sun said “the find of the season is Jack Smith, who is playing only his second year [of seniors] in the game. He started last year as a five eighth, and is now a full-back. In three matches he kicked 25 goals”.{{cite news | title = The Kiwis Are Here | page = 22 | publisher = The Sun (Sydney, NSW : 1910 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-05 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article231035830 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} [[File:Jack Smith in hat.png|thumb|right]]He was further talked up in The Labor Daily on June 6 as being “expected to develop into one of the starts of the side” and that he “kicks goals from anywhere”.{{cite news | title = Kiwis' Hopes High | page = 12 | publisher = The Labor Daily (Sydney, NSW : 1924 - 1938) | date = 1938-06-06 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-page25567014 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} [[File:J. Smith being tackled at practice by Jack Hemi.png|thumb|left]]In the same newspaper there was also a photograph of Smith being tackled at training by [[Jack Hemi]].{{cite news | title = Like His Uncle, A Hurdler | page = 12 | publisher = The Labor Daily (Sydney, NSW : 1924 - 1938) | date = 1938-06-07 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article236407014 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} [[File:Smith, Brown and Redwood at horse races.png|thumb|right]]He was also photographed at the Rosebury horse races.{{cite news | title = The Kiwi's Try Their Luck at another game | page = 8 | publisher = Wairarapa Times-Age | date = 1938-06-29 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WAITA19380629.2.92 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} Smith was named at full back in New Zealand’s first team to play against [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] on June 11 at the [[Sydney Cricket Ground]].{{cite news | title = Team To Play N.S.W. | page = 8 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 135 | date = 1938-06-10 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380610.2.96 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}}{{cite news | title = N.Z. League Challenge To N.S.W. Stars | page = 15 | publisher = The Sun (Sydney, NSW : 1910 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-10 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article231033438 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} The first tour match saw New Zealand lose 25-12 with 28,303 in attendance, though they were unlucky after losing [[Arthur Kay (rugby league)|Arthur Kay]] to injury when they were dominating the game. Smith “played well, but did not live up to his reputation as a goal kicker”. This was due to [[Jack Hemi]] being preferred as kicker, and he would go on to kick 32 goals compared to Smith’s five and [[John Anderson (rugby league)|John Anderson]]’s three on the tour.{{cite news | title = Unlucky | page = 17 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 144 | date = 1938-06-21 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380621.2.160 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} Smith did get some attempts at goal in the first half but missed them. During the first half Smith kicked to [[Frank Johnston (rugby league)|Frank Johnston]], and two passes later the NSW clearing kick was charged down by [[Harold Tetley]] with Arthur Kay gathering and scoring.{{cite news | title = Tour Begins; N. Zealand v N.S. Wales | page = 4 | publisher = The Telegraph (Brisbane, Qld. : 1872 - 1947) | date = 1938-06-11 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article183401460 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} He also drove NSW back with his punting on other occasions. A few minutes into the second half Smith “limped out” with an injury, though he soon came back on.{{cite news | title = League Victory To N.S.W. | page = 9 | publisher = The Sun (Sydney, NSW : 1910 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-11 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article231025305 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} The [[Truth (Sydney newspaper)|Truth (Sydney)]] newspaper wrote that “Smith had a lot to do for a full-back and looked all in at the finish. He has a most pleasing style”.{{cite news | title = Kiwis Can Take It | page = 6 | publisher = Truth (Sydney, NSW : 1894 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-12 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article169090637 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} [[The Courier-Mail]] noted that “one of the great surprises was the poor kicking of J. Smith, full-back, and J. Hemi, the winger. Both had gained reputations in New Zealand as first rate goal kickers. On Saturday Hemi had several kicks, and Smith had a couple, but they showed poor form”.{{cite news | title = Weight Told On N.Z. | page = 10 | publisher = The Courier-Mail (Brisbane, Qld. : 1933 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-13 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article41015772 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} With Smith’s first attempt he “kicked the ground and only dribbled the ball along. His second attempt was little better. Apparently Smith, only a youth, was nervous, as he was playing in his first big match”.{{cite news | title = Heroic Efforts To Stave Off Defeat | page = 15 | publisher = The Sydney Morning Herald (NSW : 1842 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-13 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article17485920 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} The Labor Daily said that “fullback Smith had a lot to do and was a very tired youth at the finish”.{{cite news | title = Weight Factor In R. League Battle | page = 11 | publisher = The Labor Daily (Sydney, NSW : 1924 - 1938) | date = 1938-06-13 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article236406712 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} While [[The Daily Telegraph (Sydney)]] wrote that he “catches the ball nicely, has a long-range punt, and is a fine tackler”.{{cite news | title = Good Grip On Game Till Loss Of Kay | page = 15 | publisher = The Daily Telegraph (Sydney, NSW : 1931 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-13 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article247251257 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} [[File:Jack Smith tackling Frank Hyde.png|thumb|left]]For their second match against the same New South Wales opponent two days later in June 13, Smith was bracketed at fullback with Hemi in the initial squad list.{{cite news | title = Changes In League Teams | page = 13 | publisher = The Sydney Morning Herald (NSW : 1842 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-13 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article17485832 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} When the final team was named Hemi played fullback with Smith on the right wing. He had replaced [[Ray Brown (New Zealand)|Ray Brown]] in that position.{{cite news | title = New Zealand Team | page = 24 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23064 | date = 1938-06-15 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380615.2.230.5 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} New Zealand won 37-18 before 18,426 spectators with Smith scoring two of New Zealand’s seven tries.{{cite news | title = New South Wales Firsts 18-37 New Zealand | publisher = Rugby League Project | url =https://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/matches/kiwis-tour-1938/game-2/new-south-wales-firsts-vs-new-zealand.html | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} Early in the match Smith was involved in “a nice New Zealand movement” with brothers [[Walter Brimble]] and [[Wilfred Brimble]], and [[Wally Tittleton]] which took play into the New South Wales 25. With New Zealand trailing 8-3 “New Zealand attacked, and in a loose movement [[Bill McNeight]] got the ball to [[Rangi Chase (rugby league, born 1918)|Rangi Chase]], who threw it to Smith, who scored in the corner”.{{cite news | title = Surprise In League Game | page = 13 | publisher = The Sun (Sydney, NSW : 1910 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-13 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article231035456 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} His other try was “the best try of the day” when “[[Jack McLeod (rugby league)|[Jack] McLeod]], [[Wally Tittleton|Tittleton]], [[Wilfred Brimble]], and McLeod again and Smith handled for the last named to cross in the corner” after having to reach out.{{cite news | title = Tables Turned | page = 17 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 138 | date = 1938-06-14 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380614.2.149 | accessdate = 2024-11-29}} In the second half William Conlon for NSW “ran hard, but lost the ball and Smith sped downfield and kicked”, then later Smith was involved with Hemi and McLeod “in a fine passing burst” with McLeod being tackled on the line. The [[Sydney Morning Herald]] said the following day that [[Clarry McNeil]] and Smith “on the wings, showed dash”.{{cite news | title = The Kiwis | page = 15 | publisher = The Sydney Morning Herald (NSW : 1842 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-14 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article17473744 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}} While [[The Referee newspaper|The Referee]] newspaper said “Smith and Tittleton quick as cats, turned errors by home backs into tries by very clever football, the second try after perfect running and passing, with the supports most timely”. And went on to add “Smith, Chase, McNeil, and Tittleton. These glittered individually and in unison. Beautiful to watch, they took advantage of many errors by home backs in ground fielding and in passing. Smith and Chase were particular stars on this occasion”. And that he “shaped nicely, kicking strongly” during the match.{{cite news | title = Fascinating Rugby Exponents | page = 14 | publisher = Referee (Sydney, NSW : 1886 - 1939) | date = 1938-06-16 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article127505943 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}}{{cite news | title = Best N.Z. League Team Ever In Australia | page = 14 | publisher = Referee (Sydney, NSW : 1886 - 1939) | date = 1938-06-16 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article127505919 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}} [[File:New Zealand RL team to play Far North Coast in 1938.jpg|450px|right|New Zealand RL team to play Far North Coast in 1938]]Following the game the team left for [[Lismore, New South Wales|Lismore]] on June 14 on a special division of the [[North Coast Mail]].{{cite news | title = Easy Win | page = 9 | publisher = Newcastle Morning Herald and Miners' Advocate (NSW : 1876 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-14 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article133623359 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}} Smith was named at fullback for the June 15 game against [[Sunshine Coast Gympie Rugby League|North Coast]].{{cite news | title = Will Play In Lismore Today | page = 8 | publisher = Northern Star (Lismore, NSW : 1876 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-15 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article94567732 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}} New Zealand won 23-2 on a “rain-soaked field” with heavy showers falling during the match with 2,200 spectators present. During the first half with the North Coast attacking in New Zealand territory “Smith cleared with a long penalty”. A while later J. Kilroy broke through but “was brought down by Smith, who snapped up the ball and New Zealand crashed through the defence; but with the whole field to themselves [[Walter Brimble|[Walter] Brimble]] passed forward to [[John Anderson (rugby league)|Anderson]] and a splendid chance was lost”. The tackle was described in more detail with Kilroy running “from the 25 to the centre field flag and was drawing away from the full back Smith. The last named, however hurled himself at the winger and brought him down. It was a grand tackle, but was only one of the many made by both full backs during the day”. Smith was said to have outplayed his opposite (O’Connor) but “principally because he had more opportunities”.{{cite news | title = Far North Beaten 23 Points To 2 | page = 10 | publisher = Northern Star (Lismore, NSW : 1876 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-16 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article94568038 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}} With Jack Hemi not in the side Smith had goal kicking duties and he “was an accurate goal kicker and converted four of the five New Zealand tries”. {{cite news | title = N.Z. League Team Leads Far North Coast | page = 28 | publisher = The Sun (Sydney, NSW : 1910 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-15 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article231038374 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}} Smith’s defence was good with it reported that “only sound tackling by him prevented the home side from scoring several tries”.{{cite news | title = Should Have Been Bigger | page = 28 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 140 | date = 1938-06-16 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380616.2.216 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}} [[File:Tittleton lining up a tackle on Ryrie.jpg|thumb|Smith attempting a tackle on Jack Ryrie with the caption incorrectly claiming it was Wally Tittleton.]]Smith found himself back on the wing for the fourth tour match against [[Queensland rugby league team|Queensland]] on June 18. [[File:Smith missing Ryrie who scores.png|thumb|right]]Jack Hemi coming back into the side at fullback.{{cite news | title = Queensland Match Today | page = 15 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23067 | date = 1938-06-18 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380618.2.99 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}} New Zealand was well beaten 31-11 at the [[The Gabba|Woolloongabba]] (Brisbane Cricket Ground) before 12,000 spectators. Smith scored New Zealand’s only try.{{cite news | title = Heavy Defeat | page = 11 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23068 | date = 1938-06-20 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380620.2.93 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}} New Zealand was badly beaten for possession in the scrums and the backs were starved of the ball. The Truth newspaper reported that “not enough was seen of the threes to rate 'em or slate 'em, but we liked Rangi Chase at centre and Jack Smith winger best. Given more ball and opportunity – wacko!”.{{cite news | title = Kiwis Came A Cropped Against Queensland | page = 6 | publisher = Truth (Sydney, NSW : 1894 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-19 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article169093912 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}} [[File:Jack Smith about to tackle Bates of Queensland.png|thumb|left]]Smith’s try came early in the second half with New Zealand trailing 18-6. Queensland fullback “whittle took the ball from Tetley’s toe, but Smith saw an open field ahead when he gathered the ball as it rebounded from a colleague”.{{cite news | title = Our Forwards Outclassed New Zealand | page = 9 | publisher = Queensland Times (Ipswich, Qld. : 1909 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-20 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article115612674 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}} [[File:J Smith tackling Jack Bates photo 2.png|thumb|left]]The Queensland wingers Ronald Cooper, and Jack Bates were said to have “more than held their own with Smith and [Ray] Brown”.{{cite news | title = Match Was Disappointing/Reardon Should Be Played Centre | page = 20 | publisher = The Telegraph (Brisbane, Qld. : 1872 - 1947) | date = 1938-06-20 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article183486972 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}} The Telegraph (Brisbane) said that all the wingers were starved of the ball though “occasionally we saw flashes of brilliance from Kiwi winger Smith, and [Tommy] Chase in the centre. But their opportunities were so rare that it would be difficult to judge their real ability. Smith has plenty of pace, and a good swerve, and had he been given room in which to move he would have made his own openings. But for the most part when he did get the ball he found himself up against a solid defence – solid in both numbers and virility – and he suffered severe punishment”.{{cite news | title = Kiwis Gave Maroons Too Much Latitude | page = 20 | publisher = The Telegraph (Brisbane, Qld. : 1872 - 1947) | date = 1938-06-20 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article183486975 | accessdate = 2024-11-30}} It was also said that “Smith on the left flank showed pace and nippiness in a couple of scuttling bursts along touch, but was not supported, and the pass inside (generally a perhapser) was not taken”.{{cite news | title = New Zealanders Outplayed By Fiery Forwards | page = 14 | publisher = Referee (Sydney, NSW : 1886 - 1939) | date = 1938-06-23 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article127497581 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} It was reported by manager Jack Redwood in the New Zealand newspapers that Smith “was troubled with a sore leg” though it was not mentioned when he had picked up this injury with the implication being that it may have occurred in the first match of the tour.{{cite news | title = Early Reverse Suffered | page = 15 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23070 | date = 1938-06-22 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380622.2.133 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} Smith was named in a 15 man squad for the next match against [[Western Clydesdales|Toowoomba]] on June 22. He was ultimately named to start, this time in the centre three-quarter position.{{cite news | title = Side To Play Toowoomba | page = 9 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23069 | date = 1938-06-21 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380621.2.94 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} New Zealand won 12-11 before a crowd of 2,500 at the [[Clive Berghofer Stadium|Toowoomba Athletic Ground]].{{cite news | title = Kiwis Meet Downs 13 at Toowoomba | page = 15 | publisher = The Telegraph (Brisbane, Qld. : 1872 - 1947) | date = 1938-06-22 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article183487932 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} Their sixth tour match was a second game against Queensland at the Woolloongabba once more on June 25. Smith was moved back to full back to enable Jack Hemi to come up into the centre three-quarter position.{{cite news | title = Return Queensland Match | page = 17 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23072 | date = 1938-06-24 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380624.2.211 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}}{{cite news | title = Kiwis Make Drastic Alterations In Team | page = 8 | publisher = The Telegraph (Brisbane, Qld. : 1872 - 1947) | date = 1938-06-23 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article183484785 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} New Zealand lost again though by the narrower score of 21-12 before a crowd of 7,000. Smith scored one of New Zealand’s two tries and they led 12-5 at halftime before tiring in the second half.{{cite news | title = Tired In Second Spell | page = 16 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 149 | date = 1938-06-27 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380627.2.178 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} Smith “eclipsed all other scoring efforts with a tricky run through the defence after fielding a punt at midfield. He received a great ovation as not a defender laid a hand on him. He tackled splendidly all day and held his own with [[Gordon Whittle|[Gordon] Whittle]], the Kangaroo fullback”.{{cite news | title = Queensland Wins | page = 11 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23074 | date = 1938-06-27 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380627.2.124 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} At one stage Ronald Cooper, the Queensland wing beat Rangi Chase and then beat Smith to score a try. Smith’s try was described in more detail saying it “was a centre movement, and was one of the cleverest efforts of the two Queensland matches. Smith slipped through the defence in amazing fashion, swirling away from tacklers with the grace of a stage dancer”.{{cite news | title = Kiwis In Lead | page = 15 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 156 | date = 1938-07-05 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380705.2.136.1 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} Smith had received the ball after a scrum close to the Queensland tryline saw [[Jack Reardon (rugby league)|Jack Reardon]] punt the ball downfield. After Smith gathered the ball ran to the Queensland three quarter line and turned “his back on them as though to pass back to his supporters, he pivoted and went through an open gate to touch down” after “leaving man after man standing as if he was glued to the sport” with Hemi converting to give New Zealand a 9-4 lead.{{cite news | title = N.Z. Much More Impressive | page = 4 | publisher = The Telegraph (Brisbane, Qld. : 1872 - 1947) | date = 1938-06-25 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-page19926241 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}}{{cite news | title = Queensland Makes Grand Revovery | page = 6 | publisher = The Courier-Mail (Brisbane, Qld. : 1933 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-27 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article41003590 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} In final comments on the game the Telegraph (Brisbane) said that Gordon “Whittle did not impress as much as his rival, Smith”.{{cite news | title = Kiwis Leave Behind Them Quite Good Impression | page = 19 | publisher = The Telegraph (Brisbane, Qld. : 1872 - 1947) | date = 1938-06-27 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article183479830 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} New Zealand now traveled to [[Tamworth, New South Wales|Tamworth]] to play a [[Group 4 Rugby League|NSW Group 4]] side on June 29. Smith was retained at fullback and kicked a goal in New Zealand’s 26-15 win. {{cite news | title = Another Win | page = 27 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 152 | date = 1938-06-30 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380630.2.227.1 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} His successful kick was a conversion of their second try by [[Billy Glynn (rugby league)|Billy Glynn]].{{cite news | title = N.Z. League Win At Tamworth | page = 26 | publisher = The Daily Telegraph (Sydney, NSW : 1931 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-30 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article247252772 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} Smith was said to have been “always sound” at fullback and his fitness was better than his opponent, G. Smith’s.{{cite news | title = Kiwis Defeat Group Team | page = 9 | publisher = The Courier-Mail (Brisbane, Qld. : 1933 - 1954) | date = 1938-06-30 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article41010195 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} [[File:J Smith being tackled by C. Hazleton.png|thumb|left]]New Zealand’s penultimate tour match was against [[Newcastle Rebels|Newcastle]] at the [[Newcastle Number 1 Sports Ground|Newcastle Sports Ground]] on July 2. Smith was named in the 16 man squad which featured 8 backs.{{cite news | title = Rugby League Strong Team | page = 23 | publisher = Newcastle Morning Herald and Miners' Advocate (NSW : 1876 - 1954) | date = 1938-07-01 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article133138662 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} He was ultimately named on the wing with Hemi at fullback and scored one of New Zealand’s six tries in a 30-19 victory before 5,000 spectators.{{cite news | title = Kiwi's Easy Win | page = 16 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 155 | date = 1938-07-04 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380704.2.226.1 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} Newcastle won the majority of the scrums and as a result the New Zealand halves [[Wilfred Brimble|Wilfred]] and [[Walter Brimble|Walter]] Brimble received very little ball and “wingers Jack Smith and Ray Brown” were starved of the ball.{{cite news | title = Kiwis Home On the Bit At Newcastle | page = 6 | publisher = Truth (Sydney, NSW : 1894 - 1954) | date = 1938-07-03 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article169089980 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} His try came “after weak efforts by the Newcastle backs” saw him cross near the corner.{{cite news | title = N.Z. Forward Tactics Beat Newcastle League Team | page = 23 | publisher = The Sun (Sydney, NSW : 1910 - 1954) | date = 1938-07-03 | url =http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article229133463 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} In the lead up [[Joe Cootes]], [[Bill McNeight]], and [[Rangi Chase (rugby league, born 1918)|Rangi Chase]] combined with Smith before he crossed.{{cite news | title = Disappointing Match | page = 3 | publisher = Newcastle Morning Herald and Miners' Advocate (NSW : 1876 - 1954) | date = 1938-07-04 | url = http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article133150653 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} For the final game of the tour against [[City New South Wales rugby league team|Sydney]] at the [[Sydney Cricket Ground]] on July 6 Smith was chosen in the unaccustomed position of halfback. The match was drawn 19-19. He was initially named in an 8 man backline on the left wing.{{cite news | title = New Zealand Players | page = 7 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23081 | date = 1938-07-05 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380705.2.43 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} Smith was shown as playing at first five eighth in some team lineups with [[Wilfred Brimble]] at halfback.{{cite news | title = Kiwi's Last Game Of The Tour | page = 11 | publisher = The Newcastle Sun (NSW : 1918 - 1954) | date = 1938-07-06 | url = http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article167303824 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} However in the most detailed match description of the game, in the Sydney Morning Herald, they stated that “in the first half New Zealand tried J. Smith – full-back and winger in earlier matches – as half-back, but after the interval [[Wilfred Brimble|W.P. Brimble]] was the half, with [[Wally Tittleton|Tittleton]] as five eighth and Smith on the wing”.{{cite news | title = Drawn Match | page = 15 | publisher = The Sydney Morning Herald (NSW : 1842 - 1954) | date = 1938-07-07 | url = http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article17484240 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} [[File:AORANGI in dry dock (8356061448).jpg|thumb|Aorangi in dry dock in Sydney in the 1940s]]With the tour now complete the New Zealand side arrived back in Auckland on board the [[MV Aorangi (1924)|Aorangi]] on the morning of July 11. In comments about the tour from one of the managers, Jack Redwood, “spoke well of J. Smith, the young North Shore player, who was fielded in every match” and said his defense was impressive. While [[Thomas McClymont|Scotty McClymont]] mentioned two highlights of the tour were Jack Hemi’s 65 yard penalty goal in [[Brisbane]], “and a try by J. Smith in the second match against Queensland. Smith went clean through the Queensland backs with a waltzing run that had everyone baffled”.{{cite news | title = Kiwis Return | page = 11 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 161 | date = 1938-07-11 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380711.2.116 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}}{{cite news | title = Valuable Experience Gained | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23087 | date = 1938-07-12 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380712.2.165 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} [[File:Tommy Chase kicking the ball for Auckland v NZ in 1938.png|thumb|Tommy Chase kicking with Jack Smith attempting to charge it down.]]New Zealand was to play a final match after their return against [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]] at Carlaw Park on July 16. Smith was named on the wing.{{cite news | title = Next Saturday/Auckland V. New Zealand | page = 16 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 161 | date = 1938-07-11 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380711.2.167 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} Auckland won the game 21 to 13 with Smith scoring an intercept try for New Zealand. The Auckland Star wrote that Smith “who went on tour at full-back, has materialised into a dashing wing of the opportunist kind”.{{cite news | title = Auckland On Top | page = 12 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 167 | date = 1938-07-18 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380718.2.140 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} His try came after he intercepted a pass from Bill Glover and crossed for New Zealand’s first try after he out sprinted [[Verdun Scott]] in a race to the corner. Smith again intercepted a pass later on and showed “individual cleverness”. Also early in the match he raced 40 yards and passed to Wilfred Brimble who sent the ball to Wally Tittleton but he dropped in and lost the try.{{cite news | title = Dominion Team | page = 13 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23092 | date = 1938-07-18 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380718.2.158.1 | accessdate = 2024-12-02}} ====Return to North Shore==== With Smith’s New Zealand obligations now complete he returned to his [[North Shore Albions|North Shore]] club side. He played in their 7-5 loss to Ponsonby on July 23 in round 14 of the championship and converted Bennetts try.{{cite news | title = League Matches | page = 13 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 173 | date = 1938-07-25 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380725.2.155 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} In the second half at fullback he “repeatedly saved his team from strong Ponsonby forward attacks” in a ground described as a “quagmire”.{{cite news | title = Backs Play Well | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23098 | date = 1938-07-25 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380725.2.147 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} He gave “one of the best displays in the match”, and his “positional play had the hall-mark of real class. Clever kicking for the gap caused Ponsonby endless trouble, and it was one of the best phases of tactical play for the occasion”.{{cite news | title = Forwards' Day/Playing To Plan | page = 17 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 174 | date = 1938-07-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380726.2.185 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} In their round 17 loss to Newton 13-10, Smith kicked a conversion and a penalty. Early in the game “good play by J. Smith gave [[Len Scott|L. Scott]] a chance, but he was tackled a few feet from the Newton line”. Then a while later “Smith raced into Newton territory before passing to Zane, and Hammill scored a fine try”.{{cite news | title = Newton's Narrow Margin | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23116 | date = 1938-08-15 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380815.2.158 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} The following week North Shore had a bye and traveled to [[Whangarei]] to play a combined Whangarei side at Jubilee Park. North Shore won 33-0 with Smith kicking five conversions and a penalty.{{cite news | title = Matches In Whangarei | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23122 | date = 1938-08-22 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380822.2.163 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} He started at centre but switched with [[Verdun Scott]] at fullback near the end of the game. His “kicking was a feature of the game, the New Zealand representative piloting the ball between the uprights from alarming angles with uncanny accuracy. He missed only two kicks during the day”.{{cite news | title = Combined Team Outclassed | page = 9 | publisher = Northern Advocate | date = 1938-08-22 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NA19380822.2.96 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} [[The Northern Advocate]] wrote: “star of the team was Jack Smith, who was one of the Kiwis’ most useful members in the recent Australian tour. While on tour Smith was available for any position – half, five-eighth, centre, wing, or full-back – and local fans saw some of his versatility, as although he played the greater part at centre he was also seen at full-back. Smith’s accurate goal-kicking was an indication of how many points can be piled up from this source”.{{cite news | title = North Shore Seniors; Sparkling Display | page = 2 | publisher = Northern Advocate | date = 1938-08-24 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NA19380824.2.4 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} In round 1 of the Roope Rooster North Shore travelled to Prince Edward Park in [[Papakura]] to play the [[Papakura Sea Eagles|Papakura]] side. North Shore won 5-3 with Smith kicking a crucial penalty goal in the first half.{{cite news | title = Win For North Shore | page = 8 | publisher = Franklin Times, Volume XXVII, Issue 101 | date = 1938-08-29 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/FRTIM19380829.2.34.8 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} They beat [[Marist Saints|Marist Old Boys]] 8-2 in round 2. He and G. Brown “did fine work in defence, especially Smith, whose covering was very thorough”. He converted a try to Souter with “a great kick” and at centre three-quarter, “was a thorn in the side of the opposition, and repeatedly saved his team with good positional play and excellent defence”.{{cite news | title = Success Deserved | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23134 | date = 1938-09-05 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380905.2.175 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} He then kicked two penalties in a 20-7 semi final loss to [[Richmond Bulldogs|Richmond]] on September 10. Their try to Brown came after Smith had “raced well into Richmond territory”.{{cite news | title = Backs In Form | page = 16 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23140 | date = 1938-09-12 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380912.2.166 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} Their final game of the season was a Phelan Shield defeat to Papakura on September 17 by 16 points to 12. Smith converted both of Brown’s tries and kicked a penalty while playing “a sound game at fullback”.{{cite news | title = Papakura Wins | page = 18 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXV, Issue 23146 | date = 1938-09-19 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380919.2.160 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} Along with Brown, V. Scott, Smith was “the best of the backs”, with Smith “showing a lot of pace on occasions when running for position”.{{cite news | title = Richmond Get Bird | page = 17 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXIX, Issue 223 | date = 1938-09-20 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380920.2.167 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} ===1939 season=== [[File:Wilfred Brimble passing with Jack Smith in the background of preseason training.png|thumb|left|Mixed club preseason training in March with [[Wilfred Brimble]] passing from a scrum. Jack Smith is in the background.]]North Shore opened their season on April 15 in [[1939 Auckland Rugby League season|1939]] with a 26-8 win over Ponsonby. Smith converted all four of their tries and kicked three penalties and “played an outstanding game” at fullback.{{cite news | title = Smith's Goal Kicking | page = 13 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 89 | date = 1939-04-17 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390417.2.119.2 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} He “was always sound”, “and often extracted his side from difficulties”.{{cite news | title = North Shore's Success | page = 13 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23323 | date = 1939-04-17 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390417.2.133.2 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} Smith “excelled as a goal kicker… and did splendid all-round work”. The Herald went on to say he “is a fine positional player, and knows how to open up the game for his three-quarters. He lacks nothing in defence, in spite of his light weight”.{{cite news | title = Fine Fullback Play | page = 20 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23325 | date = 1939-04-19 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390419.2.195 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} He kicked a penalty in their 7-0 win over Richmond a week later where he was “the best back on the ground”. With his defence being “superb” and he “ran for position and made a wonderful opening to make the try which put Shore firmly in the lead”.{{cite news | title = Shore Win Again | page = 16 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 95 | date = 1939-04-24 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390424.2.162 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} He gathered “the ball on the full in Richmond’s half [and] beat three or four opponents and then centre-kicked cleverly” after he had been angling for the corner with the Richmond forwards in pursuit. It allowed Edgar Morgan so score a simple try. His and Verdun Scott’s kicking “was much better than that of the Richmond players”. The newspaper went on to say that Smith “played an outstanding game at fullback. His handling and kicking were faultless, and he showed plenty of dash in racing up to start attacks. His fine grubber line kicks often gained valuable ground. On this form Smith seems assured of a place in the New Zealand team to tour England”.{{cite news | title = A Good Win | page = 17 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23329 | date = 1939-04-24 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390424.2.175 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}}{{cite news | title = Football Personalities – Another Good League Full-Back | page = 13 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 98 | date = 1939-04-28 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390428.2.121 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} North Shore was well beaten by [[Manukau Magpies|Manukau]] in round 4 by 23 to 7. Smith kicked a conversion and penalty. He was North Shore’s “best back” and was said to be “the outstanding league fullback in Auckland at the moment”.{{cite news | title = Good Manukau Form | page = 15 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 100 | date = 1939-05-01 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390501.2.169 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} He “had a hard afternoon and came through with credit. One blemish was when he stopped [[Jack Brodrick|[Jack] Brodrick]] and then allowed the Manukau forward to break away and score”.{{cite news | title = Splendid Team Work | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23334 | date = 1939-05-01 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390501.2.154 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} Smith was unable to play the following week against Marist after sustaining an injury.{{cite news | title = Defeat Of North Shore | page = 16 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23340 | date = 1939-05-08 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390508.2.160 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} Smith returned to play in round 6 in their 19-12 win over Newton where he converted their three tries and kicked two penalties.{{cite news | title = Smith's Goal Tally | page = 16 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 112 | date = 1939-05-15 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390515.2.153 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} He kicked three more goals in a 15-12 win against [[Mount Albert Lions|Mount Albert]] on May 20. Smith “played another great game at fullback… and is the best in the competition at present”.{{cite news | title = Win Well Deserved | page = 15 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 118 | date = 1939-05-22 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390522.2.150 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} During the second half he “saved a certain try when [[Clarry McNeil|[Clarry] McNeil]] was grounded just short of the line”. Smith also showed “safe hands” when fielding kicks, and “played a fine all-round game”.{{cite news | title = Fast Forwards | page = 15 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23352 | date = 1939-05-22 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390522.2.159 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} The New Zealand Herald named Smith on the wing in a hypothetical Auckland representative side at this stage of the season.{{cite news | title = Auckland Strong | page = 22 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23354 | date = 1939-05-24 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390524.2.198.4 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} His good kicking form continued in a 21-0 win over Papakura on May 27 where he kicked three conversions, two penalties, and a rare [[drop goal]] on the number 2 field at [[Carlaw Park]]. The Auckland Star said he “was in his brightest kicking mood. Indeed, it was largely Smith’s kicking which made the margin between the teams so wide”.{{cite news | title = Smith's Fine Kicking | page = 12 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 124 | date = 1939-05-29 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390529.2.143 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} Two of his goals were described as “remarkable” with one coming from 40 yards out, and his field goal was kicked from near half-way. In addition his “general play was good all round”.{{cite news | title = North Shore's Easy Win | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23358 | date = 1939-05-29 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390529.2.149 | accessdate = 2024-12-03}} With the representative games approaching the Auckland Star speculated that “J. Smith, the North Shore player, is the logical choice at full-back, as at the moment he is the outstanding full-back in the game”.{{cite news | title = Leaven Of Youth In The First League Selection | page = 15 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 125 | date = 1939-05-30 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390530.2.155 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} He scored a try and kicked 4 goals in North Shore’s 26-16 win over [[City Rovers|City]] on June 3. His goal kicking was described as “splendid” and “twice Smith was responsible for tries”. He “started a movement and scored a try from fullback, a rare feat”. Had it “not been for the splendid defence of Smith, the scores would have been much closer”.{{cite news | title = North Shore Succeeds | page = 15 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23364 | date = 1939-06-05 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390605.2.155 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} Prior to the match he had been named at fullback for the Auckland Pākehā side to play Auckland Māori on Monday, June 5.{{cite news | title = Pakehas To Play Maoris | page = 15 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23359 | date = 1939-05-30 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390530.2.175 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} The Herald wrote “in the selection of the fullback, J. Smith, of North Shore, was the first choice for the Auckland team, and he will be opposed by [[George Nēpia]], who us fit again after an injury to his arm some weeks ago. Against [[Waikato rugby league team|South Auckland]] last Sunday, Nepia played a brilliant game, and Smith will need to be right on his game to overshadow the famous Maori fullback”.{{cite news | title = Several Likely Players | page = 20 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23360 | date = 1939-05-31 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390531.2.206.3 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} As it so happened Smith was unable to play owing to an injury and his place at fullback was taken by his team mate [[Verdun Scott]].{{cite news | title = An Outstanding Back | page = 20 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23366 | date = 1939-06-07 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390607.2.192 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} Smith was available to play 5 days later on June 17 in North Shore’s 16-8 win over Ponsonby. He scored another try and kicked a conversion and a penalty. For his try he “showed splendid anticipation when he raced inside V. Scott, the centre, and caught the Ponsonby backs hopelessly out of position”. Overall he “was outstanding and saved his team by excellent fielding and good line-kicking”.{{cite news | title = North Shore's Success | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23376 | date = 1939-06-19 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390619.2.145 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} He kicked three goals in a 9-8 win against Richmond a week later and “stood out” along with Richmond’s [[Dave Solomon (rugby)|Dave Solomon]].
