Smith “played the smooth, polished game which is expected of the best league fullback in New Zealand, fielding cleanly, placing his kicks with judgment, tackling unerringly and often running his supports into better position”.{{cite news | title = Shore Forwards Great | page = 13 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 148 | date = 1939-06-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390626.2.144 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} Smith kicked a conversion against Manukau in their round 13 win by 19 to 5. He was opposed by Manukau fullback George Nēpia and their play was “of a high standard”.{{cite news | title = Forwards Excel | page = 13 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 154 | date = 1939-07-03 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390703.2.153.2 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} Nepia claimed “a distinct advantage in duels of range-kicking with Smith”.{{cite news | title = Fine Forward Play | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23388 | date = 1939-07-03 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390703.2.175 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} ====New Zealand trials and New Zealand selection==== On July 7 the [[North Island rugby league team|North Island]] side was chosen for their inter-island match along with NZ Possibles and Probables sides to play [[curtain raiser]] at Carlaw Park. Smith was selected in the Possibles side at fullback.{{cite news | title = Rugby League | page = 13 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 158 | date = 1939-07-07 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390707.2.120 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} Smith captained the Possibles side and kicked 4 goals in their 31-17 loss.{{cite news | title = Trial Games | page = 13 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 160 | date = 1939-07-10 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390710.2.155.3 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} He “was very safe at fullback… and his kicking was an asset”.{{cite news | title = Win For Probables | page = 13 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23394 | date = 1939-07-10 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390710.2.161.2 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} Smith was named in the final trial match which was between Probable and Possible sides once more three days later on July 11.{{cite news | title = Final Trial Match | page = 13 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 160 | date = 1939-07-10 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390710.2.155.2 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} He kicked two goals in a 27-18 loss. The Auckland Star wrote that “there was a duel between J. Smith and T. Shaw, of South Auckland [Waikato], the fullbacks. Smith was faultless in his handling and placed his kicks with fine judgment”.{{cite news | title = Final Trial/Good Forwards | page = 18 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 162 | date = 1939-07-12 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390712.2.140 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} He was “outstanding, and his clean fielding was a feature of the game”.{{cite news | title = League Trial | page = 11 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23396 | date = 1939-07-12 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390712.2.36 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} Then after a “nearly six hour” deliberation on the night of July 11 the New Zealand selectors, [[Thomas McClymont|Scotty McClymont]], Jack Redwood, and [[Jim Amos (rugby league)|Jim Amos]] selected 18 of the 26 required for the tour. Smith was named as one of the two fullbacks along with [[Jack Hemi]]. In comments on his selection the Herald said Smith “at fullback, is one of the most discussed players in the game. He has played faultless football this season, and in Australia last year with the New Zealand team he played good games at five-eighths and on the wing”.{{cite news | title = Players' Careers | page = 15 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23396 | date = 1939-07-12 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390712.2.94 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} He played in one final game for North Shore before the teams departure, an 18-5 loss to Marist where he kicked one penalty. He shared the fullback position with Verdun Scott who had also been selected for the tour.{{cite news | title = Marist Play Well | page = 14 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 166 | date = 1939-07-17 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390717.2.144.1 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} He had received a minor injury early in the game and moved to centre three-quarter with he and Scott swapping places.{{cite news | title = Surprise Victory | page = 15 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23400 | date = 1939-07-17 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390717.2.156 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} ====New Zealand tour of England==== [[File:NZ Team photo in 1939.jpg|thumb|left|The New Zealand rugby league team to tour England in 1939-40.]][[File:J Smith departure photo 1939.png|thumb|Smith at his departure with an unidentified woman. Possibly Mrs. C.E. Szeibalick.]]In an Evening Star (Wellington) piece they wrote “when Jack Smith, Kiwi full-back reaches the North of England he will meet for the first time his well-known uncle, George Smith, regarded as one of the greatest wingers ever to play for New Zealand. The nephew promises to reach the same heights. Only 20 when he represented New Zealand last year, Jack Smith won golden Press notices from Australian critics, and his form in Auckland this year made him early acclaimed as certain to go to England. Smith is equally at home on the wing and at centre, as he is at fullback – altogether an extremely useful fellow to have in any side”.{{cite news | title = For First Time | page = 1 | publisher = Evening Star, Issue 23325 | date = 1939-07-22 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ESD19390722.2.229.2 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} Smith was working at the Self Help Co-op., Limited at the time and on the Saturday night before his departure they held a staff banquet at the New Savoy Hall. There they fair welled him and presented him “with a well filled wallet”.{{cite news | title = League Team Member | page = 9 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 172 | date = 1939-07-24 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390724.2.88 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} The Auckland members of the side got the Express train to Wellington on July 26 with photographs appearing of some of the touring group including Smith with a woman.{{cite news | title = Farewells Said To Auckland Members of the New Zealand Rugby League Team | page = 10 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23409 | date = 1939-07-27 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390727.2.27.5 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} The team departed [[Wellington]] for [[London]] on board the [[RMS Rangitiki]] in Tourist Class on Friday July 28.{{cite news | title = Shipping Mails And Weather | page = 4 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVI, Issue 23412 | date = 1939-07-31 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390731.2.7 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} The tour was to be cut very short however as [[Great Britain]] declared war on [[Germany]]. Smith did not play in the first tour match against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] on September 2. With war then declared the second match against [[Hull Kingston Rovers|Hull KR]] was cancelled. The team managed to organise to play [[Dewsbury Rams|Dewsbury]] on September 9 just prior to their return to New Zealand. Smith played on the wing in the match which New Zealand won 22-10 at [[Crown Flatt]] (Desbury) before a crowd of 6,200.{{cite news | title = Dewsbury 10 – 22 New Zealand | publisher = Rugby League Project | url =https://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/matches/kiwis-tour-1939/game-3/dewsbury-vs-new-zealand.html | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} [[File:C.E. Jack Smith portrait.png|thumb]]By the time they returned to New Zealand the domestic competition was over though there was a brief mention of him playing in a cricket match in December. A portrait photograph of him was published alongside it saying he scored 63 [[not out]] and 60 for [[Waipā District|Waipā]] in a match which was part of the Auckland Cricket Associations Country Week tournament at the [[Auckland Domain]].{{cite news | title = C.E. (Jack) Smith | page = 11 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXX, Issue 304 | date = 1939-12-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19391226.2.119.1 | accessdate = 2024-12-04}} ===1940 season=== With World War 2 underway the representative program was scaled back with the main focus being on the club competition in Auckland. Smith played 18 games for North Shore in the [[1940 Auckland Rugby League season|1940]] season, scoring six tries and kicking 46 goals. He also played one game for the Auckland Pākehā side. The Auckland Star at the start of the season mentioned that North Shore would again have his services in their senior side.{{cite news | title = Championship Commences | page = 10 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXI, Issue 93 | date = 1940-04-19 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19400419.2.125 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} His first game was in round 1 of the championship in a 16-10 win over [[Mount Albert Lions|Mount Albert]] where he scored a try, and kicked a conversion and four penalties in the main match at Carlaw Park on April 20. The Star wrote “J. Smith, the New Zealand representative, who went to England last year in the Dominion team, registered a fine performance in gathering 13 of Shore’s 16 points, his tally being a try and five goals. He played at centre with Verdun Scott at fullback and “they rose to every call made on them and were superlative in defence”.{{cite news | title = Hard Fought | page = 11 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXI, Issue 95 | date = 1940-04-22 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19400422.2.133 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} One of his penalty kicks early was “a brilliant” kick “from just inside halfway” while he “was a dominant figure in the backs, his good positional play foiling Mount Albert’s attempts at penetration”.{{cite news | title = Fine Forward Play | page = 14 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23637 | date = 1940-04-22 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400422.2.129 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} He then kicked four more goals in a 20-12 win over Marist. He again played centre and “revealed his consistent form” and showed “splendid defence”.{{cite news | title = Well Balanced Team | page = 11 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXI, Issue 100 | date = 1940-04-29 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19400429.2.181 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}}{{cite news | title = Marist Somewhat Unlucky | page = 11 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23642 | date = 1940-04-29 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400429.2.107 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} Three more goals followed for Smith in a 15-12 loss to Richmond in round 3. He started at centre but in the second half he was brought up to first five eighth to “keep an eye on” the brilliant young [[Abbie Graham]] who would become a Kiwi after the war in 1947.{{cite news | title = Graham Outstanding | page = 12 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXI, Issue 106 | date = 1940-05-06 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19400506.2.149 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} It was Smith and Scott who “saved North Shore repeatedly with good positional play and solid tackling” as the rest of the North Shore backs struggled to contain the likes of Graham, [[Dave Solomon (rugby)|Dave Solomon]], [[George Mitchell (rugby league)|George Mitchell]], and [[Wally Tittleton]].{{cite news | title = Well-Deserved Win | page = 11 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23648 | date = 1940-05-06 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400506.2.114 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} North Shore then beat Ponsonby 19-6 on May 11 with Smith kicking two conversions and two penalties. Smith apparently spoiled the few chances the North Shore backs got by “running into the forwards”.{{cite news | title = North Shore Not Extended | page = 12 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23654 | date = 1940-05-13 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400513.2.104 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} Smith added 13 more points to his season tally when North Shore beat [[Papakura Sea Eagles|Papakura]] in round 5 by 37 points to 8. He and Bruce Donaldson “made gaps in the defence” as their combination “at five eighths was the deciding factor. This pair have become a splendid attacking force, which should prove a big asset in future games”.{{cite news | title = Papakura Outclassed | page = 12 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23660 | date = 1940-05-20 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400520.2.109 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} Smith was then named by selectors [[Hec Brisbane]], [[Bill Cloke]], and [[Dougie McGregor]] at fullback in the Auckland Pākehā side to play Auckland Māori on June 8.{{cite news | title = Match Against Maoris | page = 5 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23666 | date = 1940-05-27 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400527.2.18 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} He ultimately didn’t play in the match and it was later revealed that he had pulled a leg muscle, most likely in the June 1 win over City.{{cite news | title = Leading Teams To Meet | page = 12 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23679 | date = 1940-06-11 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400611.2.121 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} He had kicked a solitary conversion in the game. He had “spoiled his supports’ prospects by holding on too long and running into trouble. Nevertheless, Smith, prior to being injured and retiring to the wing, gave valuable service defensively, and occasionally opened up the play for Donaldson”.{{cite news | title = Backs Handle Well | page = 4 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23672 | date = 1940-06-03 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400603.2.19 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} Despite being expected to miss their June 15 game against Newton he played though did not have kicking duties in their 12-8 win over Newton. He played in the five eighths again and he and L. Davis “concentrated on defence”.{{cite news | title = Fine Forward Play | page = 4 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23684 | date = 1940-06-17 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400617.2.18 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} Smith had resumed goal kicking again by round 9 and kicked two goals in their 12-7 win over Manukau in the main match at Carlaw Park. He was said to be “a utility back of New Zealand class” and “went brilliantly at first five eighth”.{{cite news | title = Better Blend | page = 10 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXI, Issue 154 | date = 1940-07-01 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19400701.2.137 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}}{{cite news | title = Close Decision | page = 3 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23696 | date = 1940-07-01 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400701.2.13 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} Smith was paired with Eric Chatham and Chatham was said to have “sharpened up the attack considerably. He and J. Smith appear a happier combination than any possessed formerly this season, it being noticeable in some of the earlier games that the styles of the inside backs were inclined to clash”.{{cite news | title = General Sports News | page = 5 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23696 | date = 1940-07-01 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400701.2.28 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} The following week however the two of them struggled to break through the Mount Albert defence with Smith scoring all seven of their points in a 17-7 loss. They “tried desperately to shatter the opposing defence, alternating with lateral and straight running, but their efforts were usually frustrated before the ball had reached J. Greenwood at centre”.{{cite news | title = Well-Deserved Win | page = 3 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23702 | date = 1940-07-08 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400708.2.12 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} North Shore, who were leading the competition then recorded a 7-5 win over Marist with Smith kicking a penalty. He was said to be one of their best backs along with McArthur and Wilkie, and Smith supported Verdun Scott “excellently on defence”.{{cite news | title = Leaders' Close Call | page = 3 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23708 | date = 1940-07-15 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400715.2.15 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} North Shore suffered their first loss in several weeks when Richmond defeated them 18-13. Smith kicked four goals in the loss. He was “at the moment kicking with greater accuracy than any other player in the game. He was lucky with one penalty shot. The ball struck the top side of the cross bar and then bounced over”.{{cite news | title = Smith's Four Goals | page = 10 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXI, Issue 172 | date = 1940-07-22 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19400722.2.123 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} He was “solid at first five eighths… [and] attempted to put an edge on the attack with occasional straight running, but found the defence impregnable”.{{cite news | title = Resourceful Backs | page = 3 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23714 | date = 1940-07-22 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400722.2.15 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} They lost again, 8-3 to Ponsonby with Smith playing at fullback due to an ongoing injury to Scott. He “gave a fine showing, fielding cleanly and kicking with good length and judgment. Smith’s positional play was good all through the match, and his defence was always above question”.{{cite news | title = Again A Fullback | page = 10 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXI, Issue 178 | date = 1940-07-29 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19400729.2.106 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} They won their next match 33-5 against [[Papakura Sea Eagles|Papakura]] in round 14 with Smith scoring one of their seven tries and kicking three conversions.{{cite news | title = Notable Goal Kicking | page = 10 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXI, Issue 184 | date = 1940-08-05 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19400805.2.111 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} He was again at fullback and “played an outstanding game and started many effective movements”.{{cite news | title = Comfortable Victory | page = 12 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23726 | date = 1940-08-05 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400805.2.124 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} North Shore beat City to stay in the championship race on August 10, but then on August 17 Richmond beat Marist to seal the competition with North Shore having a bye. North Shore then finished with a 13-7 loss to Manukau in the penultimate round, and a 33-15 win over Newton in the final round to finish runner up. The game against Newton was belatedly played several weeks later to find the runner up after they had finished tied with Mount Albert but had played one less game. Against Manukau he was “dependable at five eighths without being outstanding”.{{cite news | title = Spectacular Play | page = 10 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23744 | date = 1940-08-26 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400826.2.109 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} While against Newton he scored two tries and kicked four goals. With “one feature [being] the excellent play at five eighths and goal kicking of J. Smith, who scored 14 of his team’s points”. He was easily the outstanding player on the field and worried the opposition repeatedly with his strong and elusive running”.{{cite news | title = North Shore v. Newton | page = 3 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23768 | date = 1940-09-23 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400923.2.22 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} ====Auckland Pākehā v Auckland Māori==== Smith was named on the wing to play for Auckland Pākehā against Auckland Māori on August 31 at Carlaw Park.{{cite news | title = Representative Thirteens | page = 10 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXI, Issue 202 | date = 1940-08-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19400826.2.125 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} The Auckland Pākehā side won 27 to 6 before 5,000 spectators with Smith scoring two tries and kicking three penalties. A “feature of the game was the excellent display given by J. Smith, the Auckland wing-threequarter, who scored two very fine tries and kicked three goals”.{{cite news | title = Auckland Team's Victory | page = 10 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23750 | date = 1940-09-02 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400902.2.109 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} On September 14 they played Mount Albert in round 2 of the Roope Rooster knockout and lost 23-14.{{cite news | title = Advantage In Backs | page = 3 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23762 | date = 1940-09-16 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400916.2.11 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} Lindsay Simons had returned from Wellington and this forced Verdun Scott and Smith up into the three quarter line with Smith on the wing.{{cite news | title = Plenty of Fullbacks | page = 10 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXI, Issue 220 | date = 1940-09-16 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19400916.2.107 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} It was reported at this stage of the season that Smith had scored 108 total points for the season through 96 for North Shore and 12 for the Auckland Pākehā side.{{cite news | title = General Sports News | page = 3 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23768 | date = 1940-09-23 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400923.2.24 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} On September 28 North Shore again played Mount Albert, this time in round 3 of the Phelan Shield consolation knockout competition and lost again 10-9. Smith converted their only try and added two penalties.{{cite news | title = Mount Albert v. North Shore | page = 10 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23774 | date = 1940-09-30 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19400930.2.96 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} North Shore did finish the year on a high however when they beat [[Richmond Rovers|Richmond]] 15-10 to win the Stormont Shield. The Auckland Star wrote “in addition to playing a fine positional game at full back … J. Smith gave his side great service by landing three goals, two from penalty kicks, and the other a try conversion. It was a contribution which was a big factor in the Shore victory. His place kicking was good, but not successful every time, as several shots skimmed by on the wrong side of the post”.{{cite news | title = Smith's Three Goals | page = 10 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXI, Issue 238 | date = 1940-10-07 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19401007.2.115 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} While the Herald said “Smith, at fullback, gave one of the best displays seen at Carlaw Park this season. His excellent line kicking saved the forwards a lot of unnecessary running. His defence was superb”.{{cite news | title = Splendid Recovery Staged | page = 4 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVII, Issue 23780 | date = 1940-10-07 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19401007.2.13 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} At the end of 1940 tragedy struck the Smith family with the death of Jack and Dick’s old brother Richard. He jumped from a boat that had left Whangarei endeavouring to reach the shore but was never sighted again and drowned aged 31.{{cite news | title = Deaths/Smith | page = 1 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXI, Issue 304 | date = 1940-12-23 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19401223.2.5 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} ===1941 season=== Smith was named vice captain of North Shore for the [[1941 Auckland Rugby League season|1941]] season with Verdun Scott captaining the side.{{cite news | title = None | page = 6 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 223 | date = 1941-09-20 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410920.2.180.62.2 | accessdate = 2024-12-05}} He went on to play 13 matches, scoring two tries and kicking 44 goals and a drop goal. Smith was drafted into the war effort and departed late in the season, playing for North Shore intermittently as well as playing some rugby games for Army sides. He also played one game for [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]]. Their first game was a preliminary round game against Newton on April 19 which they won 21-5. The Auckland Star said “Jack Smith, one of the Kiwis in 1939, is still an elusive back, and will occupy his position as an allrounder for Shore”.{{cite news | title = Promising Talent | page = 6 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 92 | date = 1941-04-19 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410419.2.134.12 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} He was “the pick of the backs” for North Shore along with Verdun Scott.{{cite news | title = North Shore v. Newton | page = 4 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVIII, Issue 23945 | date = 1941-04-21 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410421.2.32 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} Their second preliminary round game on April 26 saw a 28-8 win against Ponsonby with Smith kicking four goals. During the second half with the score 17-8 Smith received a head injury and had to be carried off the ground.{{cite news | title = Ponies Well Beaten By North Shore at Carlaw Park | page = 2 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 97 | date = 1941-04-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410426.2.211.22 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} He was subsequently left out of the North Shore side for their opening Fox Memorial Shield championship game a week later.{{cite news | title = Shore Combination Too Good For Manukau | page = 2 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 103 | date = 1941-05-03 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410503.2.158.16.3 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} There was only one game played the following week and North Shore had the bye the week after so by the time of their second match against Ponsonby on May 24 Smith had recovered enough to play. He kicked four more goals in a 17-7 win and he, Scott, and B. McArthur covered “cleverly in the back line and regained ground by smart interception and nicely-timed kicking”.{{cite news | title = North Shore v. Ponsonby | page = 5 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVIII, Issue 23974 | date = 1941-05-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410526.2.33 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} At this time Jack’s brother [[Dick Smith (rugby league)|Dick Smith]] was reinstated to the rugby league code and rejoined the North Shore team.{{cite news | title = Maoris V. South Auckland | page = 3 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVIII, Issue 23977 | date = 1941-05-29 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410529.2.14 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} Prior to North Shore’s next match against Richmond in round 4 the Auckland Star said that “the presence of J. Smith, the New Zealand representative player in the three quarter line will add to the Shore strategy”.{{cite news | title = Unbeaten Teams For Carlaw Park, Shore And Richmond | page = 3 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 126 | date = 1941-05-30 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410530.2.19 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} Smith played at centre with Richmond winning 12-7. He kicked two penalties for the losers.{{cite news | title = Richmond Backs Show To Advantage | page = 2 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXVI, Issue 127 | date = 1941-05-31 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410531.2.136.17 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} The two teams had been unbeaten and the game saw high tempers especially among the forwards but Smith, McArthur, and [[Roy Clark (rugby league)|Roy Clarke]] were “impressive” in the backs.{{cite news | title = Richmond v. North Shore | page = 11 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVIII, Issue 23980 | date = 1941-06-02 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410602.2.117 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} He kicked six goals in their 27-3 win over Marist and “with incisive play broke through the Marist inside backs before he passed to Clark” who scored.{{cite news | title = Six Goals By Smith For North Shore | page = 2 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 133 | date = 1941-06-07 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410607.2.149.20 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} He played a “prominent attacking role” at centre and was in “exceptional kicking form, several of the shots being from difficult angles”.{{cite news | title = Six Goals For Smith | page = 4 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 134 | date = 1941-06-09 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410609.2.16 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}}{{cite news | title = North Shore v. Marist | page = 3 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVIII, Issue 23986 | date = 1941-06-09 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410609.2.17 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} In round 6 Smith scored a try and kicked a conversion and three penalties in their 16-14 win against City and showed “subtlety in the centre”.{{cite news | title = Spectacular Field Goal Saves Shore | page = 2 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 139 | date = 1941-06-14 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410614.2.154.7.4 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} The Star wrote that “in defeating City… North Shore owed a lot to J. Smith, its centre three-quarter, who in addition to maintaining goal kicking form, showed cleverness in constructive play. Smith got a very spectacular try, the result of interception in midfield. Smith worked out on a diagonal line to draw the defence, and then with a wide swerve in the opposite direction got through to score between the posts”.{{cite news | title = Smith Shows Form | page = 4 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 140 | date = 1941-06-16 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410616.2.35 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} The Herald said that his try was “spectacular” and “his play throughout revealed polish”.{{cite news | title = North Shore v. City | page = 5 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVIII, Issue 23992 | date = 1941-06-16 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410616.2.33 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} Smith kicked another four conversions in North Shore’s 32-15 win against Papakura. The game was played at the [[Ellerslie, New Zealand|Ellerslie]] Domain on June 21 and saw Smith play “well and make many fine openings”.{{cite news | title = North Shore Overshadow Papakura 32-15 | page = 2 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 145 | date = 1941-06-21 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410621.2.143.9 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}}{{cite news | title = North Shore v. Papakura | page = 3 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVIII, Issue 23998 | date = 1941-06-23 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410623.2.18 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} Five more goals followed for Smith in their 32-12 win at Carlaw Park in round 8.{{cite news | title = Shore Combination Defeats Newton | page = 1 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 151 | date = 1941-06-28 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410628.2.136 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} He played “a fine game at centre”.{{cite news | title = North Shore v. Newton | page = 5 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVIII, Issue 24004 | date = 1941-06-30 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410630.2.29 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} Smith was named in the reserves for the [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]] team to play [[Waikato rugby league team|South Auckland (Waikato)]] on July 12. It was said that he was playing as well as he did two seasons ago.{{cite news | title = Among the Reserves | page = 4 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 152 | date = 1941-06-30 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410630.2.21 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} He followed up with another impressive goal kicking effort against Mount Albert with five conversions and a penalty in their 30-8 win. He was “one of the outstanding players” with his “fielding being faultless”.{{cite news | title = Six Goals Kicked By Smith For Shore | page = 2 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 157 | date = 1941-07-05 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410705.2.133.17 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} [[Verdun Scott]] had gone off to military camp and Smith had moved back into his fullback position.{{cite news | title = Smith's Six Goals | page = 4 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 158 | date = 1941-07-07 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410707.2.39 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} [[File:North Shore 1941 July.png|thumb|left|North Shore after winning the Rukutai Shield with Smith as captain.]]While Scott was gone Smith took on the captaincy role. After winning the Rukutai Shield for leading after the first round a photograph was taken of the side at Carlaw Park. At fullback he “greatly assisted” North Shore and he “drove back the Mount Albert side with lengthy and accurate line-kicking, while his handling, tackling and positional play were first class”.{{cite news | title = North Shore v. Mount Albert | page = 5 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVIII, Issue 24010 | date = 1941-07-07 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410707.2.25 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} ====Auckland v South Auckland (Waikato)==== After being named in the reserves originally for Auckland, Smith was pulled into the side for their match against South Auckland (Waikato) on July 12 after [[Brian Riley (rugby league)|Brian Riley]] withdrew through an ankle injury.{{cite news | title = Why Chosen League Reps. Did Not Play | page = 6 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 169 | date = 1941-07-19 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410719.2.145.61 | accessdate = 2024-12-06}} Auckland won 25-14 before a crowd of 9,500 with acting prime minister [[Walter Nash]] in attendance. Smith kicked three conversions and two penalties. It was said that he and Jack Hemi were “disappointing among the Auckland backs”, “in the first half, particularly, their handling was very weak, and this gave the visitors two tries. Smith went from wing threequarter to fullback in the second half, and Hemi did better on the wing”.{{cite news | title = South Auckland Defeated | page = 5 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVIII, Issue 24016 | date = 1941-07-14 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410714.2.32 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} The following week in their round 10 match North Shore lost 22-7 to Manukau. Smith, playing at fullback kicked two goals.{{cite news | title = Manukau Eclipses North Shore In Fast Open Game | page = 2 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 169 | date = 1941-07-19 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410719.2.145.21 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} He “was not at his best at fullback, but his task was not an easy one with the backs showing weakness in handling and slowness in backing up”.{{cite news | title = Manukau v. North Shore | page = 5 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVIII, Issue 24022 | date = 1941-07-21 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410721.2.34 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} In a brief piece in the Auckland Star it was said that “Jack Smith (North Shore) headed the league point-getters after last Saturday’s play, with 69 points, {{sic|comprised |hide=y|of}} 33 goals and one try. This performance illustrates what a stalwart he has been to the leading team in the championship”.{{cite news | title = League's Best Scorers | page = 3 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 175 | date = 1941-07-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410726.2.151.38 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} ====Papakura Rugby (Army) and North Shore champions==== Smith had been drafted into the war effort during 1941 and spent time at the army camp at [[Papakura]]. While there he played the occasional game for their [[rugby union]] side and played for North Shore when he was not in camp. The Star wrote prior to the Papakura Army’s July 26 game against Grammar at [[Eden Park]] that “the Army Rugby team has a noted recruit for its match ... in J. Smith, the Auckland and New Zealand league representative. He will fill the role of fullback, a position which a number of players have filled during the season with varying success for the Army. Smith is a class player in the league game”.{{cite news | title = Two Noted Backs To Play For The Army Tomorrow | page = 4 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 174 | date = 1941-07-25 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410725.2.32 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} His Army side beat Grammar 16-12 with Smith kicking two conversions. His “line kicking saved his forwards a lot of running about” and he “was most impressive with his long range kicking”.{{cite news | title = Army v. Grammar | page = 5 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume LXXVIII, Issue 24028 | date = 1941-07-28 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410728.2.33 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}}{{cite news | title = Army Backs Go Well | page = 4 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 176 | date = 1941-07-28 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410728.2.46 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} It was reported that “in its backs North Shore has lost V. Scott and J. Smith, both representative players, who have gone to camp, and their places have been hard to fill”.{{cite news | title = Richmond To Play City In The Chief League Game | page = 4 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 180 | date = 1941-08-01 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410801.2.38 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Smith did however return to their side for their following match against Ponsonby on August 2. He kicked a goal in an 8-8 draw and “handled cleanly, kicked well for the gaps, and showed good positional play on occasions by running the shore backs into position”.{{cite news | title = Return Of Smith | page = 4 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 182 | date = 1941-08-04 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410804.2.26 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Then mid week he played for the Papakura Army side again in a match against a [[Hopuhopu Camp|Hopuhopu army camp]] side, and kicked two penalties and a conversion in a 14-10 win.{{cite news | title = Army Teams Meet | page = 2 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume 78, Issue 24038 | date = 1941-08-08 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410808.2.9 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}}{{cite news | title = Army Match | page = 16 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 185 | date = 1941-08-07 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410807.2.141 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} [[File:North Shore Fox winners 1941.png|right|425px]]Smith was unavailable to play for North Shore for several weeks with his brother [[Dick Smith (rugby league)|Dick Smith]] filling his place.{{cite news | title = North Shore's Full-Back | page = 4 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 188 | date = 1941-08-11 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410811.2.30 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Jack did return to North Shore for their round 17 win over Papakura 35-5 and he scored a try and kicked four conversions. He played fullback and “figured prominently in several back movements” and “played a sound game”.{{cite news | title = Even For Fifteen Minutes | page = 4 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 212 | date = 1941-09-08 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410908.2.32 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}}{{cite news | title = North Shore v. Papakura | page = 5 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume 78, Issue 24064 | date = 1941-09-08 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410908.2.28 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} North Shore had a one point lead in the championship with one round remaining and they secured the Fox Memorial Shield with a 19-9 win over Newton on September 13 at Carlaw Park. Smith kicked two conversions, two penalties, and a drop goal.{{cite news | title = North Shore The League Champions | page = 2 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 217 | date = 1941-09-13 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410913.2.176.16 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Interestingly the Papakura Army side also won the [[Gallaher Shield]] title for the Auckland rugby championship meaning Smith was in an extremely unique position of having played for the teams which simultaneous won the competition in two different codes. ==Smith serving in World War 2== Smith was drafted in early July 1941 and ordered to report to Drill Hall on Rutland Street in central Auckland. He was then to travel to the Papakura Army Camp for training.{{cite news | title = Drafts For Camp | page = 8 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXII, Issue 159 | date = 1945-07-08 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19410708.2.100 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Sometime around mid to late September Smith departed New Zealand for the Middle East where he joined the war effort. He was a Corporal in the Infantry Brigade and part of the Second [[New Zealand Expeditionary Force]]. It was reported from [[Cairo]], [[Egypt]] on November 20 that he played rugby for New Zealand’s Northern Infantry Training Depot against the Australian Imperial Force Unit. The New Zealand side won 24-11 with “J. Smith, a former New Zealand league player, giving a sound display at fullback”.{{cite news | title = Army Sports/Contests In Egypt | page = 2 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume 78, Issue 24155 | date = 1941-12-23 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19411223.2.24 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Then in early December a letter was received from Second Lieutenant [[Ronald Bush]] stating that he was chosen as vice-captain of the Central Rugby team that was to meet the Northern fifteen in a day or two’s time. And that the Northern team included some well known players such as [[Hubert McLean]], A. Brown, A. McBeath, Laurie Curtayne who were all Auckland representatives, with McLean also being an All Black, along with Jack Smith.{{cite news | title = General Sports | page = 4 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume 78, Issue 24145 | date = 1941-12-11 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19411211.2.12 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Smith scored all nine of the Northern Infantry sides points from kicks. Then against the Middle East Signals School team he kicked three conversions and two penalties around the new year.{{cite news | title = Camp Rugby | page = 3 | publisher = Franklin Times, Volume XXXI, Issue 3 | date = 1941-01-09 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/FRTIM19420109.2.17 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} On May 11, 1943 the New Zealand Herald reported that Smith was missing. The piece stated “corporal C.E. (Jack) Smith, reported missing, is the third son of Mrs. C.E. Sweibruck, 14 Cecil Road, Milford. He is well known in rugby league football circles, having represented New Zealand in both the fullback and wing positions. A stalwart of the Devonport club, he toured Australia with the Kiwis in 1938 and England in 1939, the latter tour being cancelled after the second match owing to the outbreak of war. Smith, who is 25 years of age was educated at the Devonport Primary School and Takapuna Grammar School, where he took a prominent part in athletics. He also played rugby for the Papakura Army team which won the Auckland championship in 1941.{{cite news | title = Footballer Missing | page = 5 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume 80, Issue 24580 | date = 1943-05-11 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19430511.2.49 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Plainly he was later found safe as in January 1944 he was named as one of six New Zealanders to play in a “Rest of Egypt” side to play the unbeaten South Africa Divisional team at [[El Alamein|Alamein]] later in the month of January.{{cite news | title = Rugby In Egypt | page = 5 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume 81, Issue 24801 | date = 1944-01-26 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19440126.2.38 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} It was then reported in early March that Smith was part of the Second N.Z.E.F. side which beat the Rest of Egypt which at this stage included some of the South African players in their unbeaten divisional team. Smith kicked a penalty goal in their 9-7 win which took place in Alexandria.{{cite news | title = Rugby Triumph/Rest Of Egypt Defeated | page = 2 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume 81, Issue 24836 | date = 1944-03-07 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19440307.2.11 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} In February of 1945 Smith played for the New Zealand Advance Base side which lost 15 to 6 to the United Services’ team which “fielded three English representatives – F. Millar, A.J. Pimblett (rugby league), and R.E. Prescott, captain of the Harlequins and England – and other representatives in L. Bennett (Wales), L. du Toit and Hayman (South Africa). Smith’s side included W. O’Byrne (Taranaki), P.H. Baker (Canterbury), and J.B. Simpson (Auckland). Smith kicked a penalty in the defeat.{{cite news | title = Two N.Z. Teams Beaten Overseas | page = 7 | publisher = Southland Times, Issue 25611 | date = 1945-03-02 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ST19450302.2.89 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Smith returned from the war in early September 1945. The boat arrived in Wellington and then Smith was part of a large contingent of returning soldiers who travelled by train to Auckland.{{cite news | title = N.Z.E.F. Men | page = 7 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXVI, Issue 210 | date = 1945-09-05 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19450905.2.91 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} He was awarded the War Medal 1939-1945 and the [[New Zealand War Service Medal]].{{cite news | title = Charles Ernest Smith | publisher = Auckland Museum | url =https://www.aucklandmuseum.com/war-memorial/online-cenotaph/record/167583?n=Charles+Ernest+Smith&from=%2Fwar-memorial%2Fonline-cenotaph%2Fsearch&ordinal=0 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} ==Return to North Shore side and North Island selection== After arriving back from the war Smith immediately resumed his rugby league career. An advertisement appeared in several newspapers by the Auckland Rugby League promoting the North Shore v Mount Albert game which featured Smith and Verdun Scott’s names. It said “Jack Smith and Verdun Scott, two of the most brilliant products of the league code have returned from overseas in fine form”.{{cite news | title = League Football | page = 10 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXVI, Issue 223 | date = 1945-09-20 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19450920.2.109.3 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} The match was a Roope Rooster semi final and North Shore won on September 22 by 17 points to 12. Smith kicked two conversions and two penalties. The Auckland Star wrote that “for the occasion North Shore put in its best team of the season, the reinforcement including Verdun Scott, J. Smith and [[Ivor Stirling]]…”.{{cite news | title = Shore Outplay Mount Albert | page = 7 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXVI, Issue 226 | date = 1945-09-24 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19450924.2.106 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Smith produced “clever tactical play” at fullback, “was in splendid kicking form”, and “revealed a lot of his former ability”.{{cite news | title = Success For North Shore | page = 3 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume 82, Issue 25315 | date = 1945-09-24 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19450924.2.10 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} North Shore then beat Richmond 22-12 in the Roope Rooster final a week later on September 9. Smith converted two tries and kicked three penalties. Late in the game he had to go off with an injury but before then he “played a faultless game at fullback. His placements were good and effective”.{{cite news | title = Roope Rooster Final | page = 3 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume 82, Issue 25321 | date = 1945-10-01 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19451001.2.19 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Smith had been selected in the reserves for the Auckland side to play South Auckland (Waikato) in Huntly but he was not required to play and instead played for North Shore in their final on the same day. Smith was then a surprise selection in the [[North Island rugby league team|North Island]] side for their inter-island match on October 6. [[Warwick Clarke]] had been the form fullback during the season but Smith was chosen ahead of him by selectors [[Thomas McClymont| Scotty McClymont]], [[Ernie Asher]], and C. Siddle.{{cite news | title = Inter-Island League | page = 7 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXVI, Issue 232 | date = 1945-10-01 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19451001.2.115 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Before a crowd of 15 to 20,000 at Carlaw Park the North Island won 18 to 8. Smith converted two of their four tries and also kicked a penalty. Much of the interest in the game was “centred in the displays of the fullbacks, J. Smith and J. Soster. Their handling of the ball was often brilliant and Smith helped to make North’s third try by joining in at the end of a run and bringing the play back to the forwards. Smith’s tactical play and knowledge of the offside rule thwarted the opposing forwards, but he had nothing on his younger West Coast rival in all-round play”.{{cite news | title = Fullback Rivalry | page = 7 | publisher = Auckland Star, Volume LXXVI, Issue 238 | date = 1945-10-08 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19451008.2.81.9 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} The Herald wrote that they both “gave high class displays and their fielding was flawless”.{{cite news | title = Fullbacks Play Well | page = 3 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume 82, Issue 25327 | date = 1945-10-08 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19451008.2.8 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Smith’s final game of the season was in the Stormont Shield final against [[Otahuhu Leopards|Otahuhu]] on October 20. North Shore won the trophy with a 15-13 win. Smith kicking two conversions and a penalty. He “played well in the first spell at fullback… but later his form was not so consistent” in a “gusty wind”.{{cite news | title = The League Code | page = 7 | publisher = New Zealand Herald, Volume 82, Issue 25339 | date = 1945-10-22 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19451022.2.74 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} ==Personal life== Jack Smith was working as a clerk at the time of his selection for the 1939 New Zealand team.{{cite news | title = Rugby League Team | page = 10 | publisher = Gisborne Herald, Volume LXVI, Issue 19998 | date = 1939-07-25 | url = https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GISH19390725.2.151 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} He was working as a grocer at the time of his enlistment in the military in July 1941. His address was listed as 20 Broadway, Auckland. His next of kin was listed as Mrs. C.E. Szeibalick and she was living on Shakespeare Rd., in Milford, Auckland.{{cite news | title = Charles Ernest Smith | publisher = Auckland Museum | url =https://www.aucklandmuseum.com/war-memorial/online-cenotaph/record/167583?n=Charles+Ernest+Smith&from=%2Fwar-memorial%2Fonline-cenotaph%2Fsearch&ordinal=0 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Jack married Jean Constance Hunter on August 24, 1946 and they had four children, a daughter and three sons (Bryan, Terry, and Graham).{{cite news | title = Births, Deaths & Marriages | publisher = New Zealand Government | url =https://www.bdmhistoricalrecords.dia.govt.nz/Search/Search?Path=querySubmit.m%3fReportName%3dMarriageSearch%26recordsPP%3d30#SearchResults | accessdate = 2024-12-07}}{{cite news | title = Rugby League Team | page = 10 | publisher = Gisborne Herald, Volume LXVI, Issue 19998 | date = 1939-07-25 | url =https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GISH19390725.2.151 | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} His son Bryan went on to become the principal of [[Papakura High School]] from 1994 to 2012.{{cite news | title = New Principal Has Big Plans | publisher = Manukau Courier | date = 2012-08-02 | url =https://www.stuff.co.nz/auckland/local-news/manukau-courier/7397957/New-principal-has-big-plans | accessdate = 2024-12-13}} The census records of 1949 showed that Jack and Jean were living at 42 King Edward Avenue and working as a clerk.{{cite news | title = North Shore General Roll | page = 239 | publisher = New Zealand Government | date = 1949-01-01 | url =https://www.ancestryinstitution.com/search/collections/1836/records/30133081?tid=83632193&pid=232476647784&ssrc=pt | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} Charles Ernest (Jack) Smith died on Thursday, September 10, 1970 at Auckland Hospital aged 52. He was managing the University rugby league senior side at the time of his death.{{cite news | title =Births, Deaths, & Marriages | publisher = New Zealand Government | url =https://www.bdmhistoricalrecords.dia.govt.nz/Search/Search?Path=querySubmit.m%3fReportName%3dDeathSearch%26recordsPP%3d30#SearchResults | accessdate = 2024-12-07}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Smith, Jack}} [[Category:1918 births]] [[Category:1970 deaths]] [[Category:20th-century New Zealand sportsmen]] [[Category:Auckland rugby league team players]] [[Category:New Zealand national rugby league team players]] [[Category:New Zealand rugby league players]] [[Category:New Zealand rugby union players]] [[Category:North Island rugby league team players]] [[Category:North Shore Albions players]] [[Category:Rugby league centres]] [[Category:Rugby league fullbacks]] [[Category:Rugby league players from Auckland]] [[Category:Rugby union players from Auckland]]
